coredata.dc:title: “Fluorescence-wavelength” label-free POCT tandem with “fluorescence-photothermal” nanobody-immunosensor for detecting BSA and β-lactoglobulin
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shijie
Nie, Linqing
Yang, Lu
Fan, Dancai
Wang, Junping
Hu, Yaozhong
Zhang, Yan
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: Two carbon dots (CDs) (λEm = 525 nm, G-CDs and λEm = 640 nm, R-CDs) were synthesized from citric acid and urea. The bovine serum albumin (BSA) responsiveness of the R-CDs was used to develop a “fluorescence-wavelength” label-free point of care testing (POCT) for the detection of the milk quality marker BSA with the detection limit (LOD) of 4.89 μg/mL for fluorescence mode and 3.38 μg/mL for wavelength mode. In addition, R-CDs were found to have hydroxyl radical (·OH)-dependent fluorescence quenching properties, and a “fluorescence-photothermal” immunosensor based on nanobodies was constructed by introducing the fluorescence signal of R-CDs@BSA and the photothermal signal of oxTMB for the detection of β-lactoglobulin (β-LG) with the LOD of 0.034 ng/mL for fluorescence mode and 0.075 ng/mL for photothermal mode. The tandem detection of POCT and immunosensor enables the simultaneous and highly sensitive detection of BSA and β-LG after only simple dilution of less than 5 µL of sample.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BSA-enhanced red fluorescent carbon dots
Fluorescence quenching in response to hydroxyl radicals
Fluorescence-wavelength dual-readout
Fluorescence-photothermal dual-readout
Bovine serum albumin
β-Lactoglobulin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pretreatment of reaction cups for immunosensores
Construction of “fluorescence-wavelength” dual-readout POCT for detecting BSA
Construction of “fluorescence-photothermal” dual-readout nanobody-immunosensor for detecting β-LG

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs. TEM images of (a) G-CDs, (b) R-CDs, and (c) R-CD@BSA; UV–vis and florescence spectra of (d) G-CDs, (e) R-CDs, and (f) R-CD@BSA; ex-em 3D-map of (g) G-CDs, (h) R-CDs, and (i) R-CD@BSA.

Performance evaluation of POCT. Images of R-CDs@BSA in daylight (left-up) and under UV lamp (left-down) after adding different concentrations of BSA and their CIE image (a); Principle of fluorescence enhancement of R-CDs@BSA (b); Fluorescence intensity of R-CDs@BSA under different pH conditions (c); Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs with different concentration of BSA and linear standard (insert) of POCT at “fluorescence” mode (d); Normalized fluorescence spectra of R-CDs with different concentration of BSA and linear standard (insert) of POCT at “wavelength” mode (e); Specificity analysis of POCT (f); Standard curves for sample detection at 640 nm (g); Recovery rate analysis of POCT at “fluorescence” mode (h); and recovery rate analysis of POCT at “wavelength” mode (i).

Performance evaluation of immunosensor. Excitation spectra and emission spectra of G-CDs and R-CDs@BSA and the absorption spectra of the oxTMB (a); Principle of synthesis of Au@Pt NPs (b); Relationship of quenching rate of G-CDs and R-CDs@BSA versus absorbance value of oxTMB (c); Calibration curve and linear standard (insert) of sELISA (d); Specificity analysis by NCISA(e); Calibration curve and linear standard (insert) of immunosensor at “fluorescence” mode (f); Temperature response to 808 nm laser (1 W cm−2) irradiation on and off of oxTMB over a period of 1300 s and IR thermal images (Insert) (g); Photothermal and colorimetric response of oxTMB at various concentrations and its images (insert) (h); And calibration curve and linear standard (insert) of immunosensor at “fluorescence” mode (i).

Schematic of POCT tandem immunosensors. Preparation process of G-CDs, R-CDs, and R-CDs@BSA (a); Detection procedure of “fluorescence-wavelength” dual-readout POCT (b); And detection procedure of “fluorescence-photothermal” immunosensor (c).
Recovery results for the addition of UHT milk to milk-free beverages (n = 3).

head.ce:title: “Fluorescence-wavelength” label-free POCT tandem with “fluorescence-photothermal” nanobody-immunosensor for detecting BSA and β-lactoglobulin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health, School of Medicine, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Food Science and Health, School of Medicine, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Food Nutrition and Safety
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors (Shijie Li and Linqing Nie) contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two carbon dots (CDs) (λEm = 525 nm, G-CDs and λEm = 640 nm, R-CDs) were synthesized from citric acid and urea. The bovine serum albumin (BSA) responsiveness of the R-CDs was used to develop a “fluorescence-wavelength” label-free point of care testing (POCT) for the detection of the milk quality marker BSA with the detection limit (LOD) of 4.89 μg/mL for fluorescence mode and 3.38 μg/mL for wavelength mode. In addition, R-CDs were found to have hydroxyl radical (·OH)-dependent fluorescence quenching properties, and a “fluorescence-photothermal” immunosensor based on nanobodies was constructed by introducing the fluorescence signal of R-CDs@BSA and the photothermal signal of oxTMB for the detection of β-lactoglobulin (β-LG) with the LOD of 0.034 ng/mL for fluorescence mode and 0.075 ng/mL for photothermal mode. The tandem detection of POCT and immunosensor enables the simultaneous and highly sensitive detection of BSA and β-LG after only simple dilution of less than 5 µL of sample.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Fluorescence quenching in response to hydroxyl radicals

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a nutritious beverage, milk is an excellent source of fat, protein and trace elements for humans and is often added to a variety of processed foods as a technical aid (Jaiswal & Worku, 2022). β-lactoglobulin (β-LG) is one of the main active proteins in milk (0.3 g/100 mL) and is responsible for the uptake and transport of hydrophobic molecules (Villa, Moura, Costa, & Mafra, 2022). Bovine serum albumin (BSA), a major component of bovine serum (3.8 g/100 mL), has also become a marker of mammary gland health and cow quality(Rezende, Ferreira, Ferreira, da Silva, do Carmo Hepanhol da Silva, Pinto, et al., 2017). In addition, both β-LG and BSA are considered to be important allergenic proteins (Jones & Burks, 2017). The development of effective tools for β-LG and BSA detecting to safeguard milk quality, identify processed milk in food and monitor the authenticity of milk allergen labels is important to reduce the risk of disease or other health risks due to poor quality milk or potential allergen intake of consumers, to effectively assess the milk content of milk-containing foods and to safeguard the health of consumers and promote the health development of the milk and dairy industry (Dalabasmaz, Ebner, & Pischetsrieder, 2017).

The main detection techniques for β-LG and BSA include high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) (He, et al., 2018; Kovalchuk & Arkhipova, 2022; Boitz, et al., 2015). Of these, ELISA is currently the method of choice due to its high sensitivity, simplicity, and low cost (Villa, Moura, Costa, & Mafra, 2022). For β-LG, a pair of antibodies is usually required to form an “antibody-analyte-antibody” sandwich system (He, Li, Gao, Tong, & Chen, 2017). Most current reports are limited to traditional combinations of monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) or polyclonal antibodies (pAbs), but pAbs as monitoring reagents suffer from poor stability due to uneven sensitivity and specificity between production batches and cannot be used for long-term accurate monitoring. Therefore, the development of better performing antibodies is essential for immunoassays. Nanobodies (Nbs) are VHH structural domains found on pure heavy chain antibodies of the Camelidae family, which are the smallest natural antibody fragments with molecular weights as low as 15 kDa(J. Zhao, et al., 2022) (Cruz-Pacheco, et al., 2023). Nbs have excellent physical, chemical and biological properties, including solubility, heat resistance and resistance to protease hydrolysis, compared to conventional antibodies. These specific properties of Nbs make them good candidates for the development of assays for various food safety risk factors. In contrast to β-LG, the unique structure of BSA makes direct probe-based identification one of the most rapid and simple means of detecting BSA (Hang, Yang, Qu, & Hua, 2014) (Rezende, et al., 2017).

Fluorescent signals have significant advantages over colourimetric signals in terms of sensitivity, accuracy and cost, both in the construction of immunosensors and in the direct detection of BSA (Chen, Li, Zhang, Xu, & Song, 2022). Carbon dots (CDs) are highly sought after and widely used due to their excellent optical properties, high biocompatibility, and low toxicity (Kang & Lee, 2019). Whereas the fluorescence emission wavelengths of CDs have so far been mostly concentrated in the blue region, the fluorescence of milk itself overlaps to some extent with the emission wavelengths of blue CDs, which may cause background interference (Lin, et al., 2022) (B. Zhao, Wang, & Tan, 2020). Although some red CDs and even multicolored CDs have been prepared by very sophisticated technical means, these CDs fluoresce strongly in organic solutions (e.g. ethanol) but poorly in aqueous solutions, which greatly limits their application in aqueous systems. In addition to fluorescent signals, photothermal signals are much easier to capture and measure and have much higher sensitivity (Xu, et al., 2021). The photothermal properties of oxTMB, a common catalytic substrate in ELISA, are also emerging as a validated detection signal (Fu, Hou, Mou, & Li, 2021). With the introduction of COVID-19 vaccination, the infrared thermometer manufacturing industry has seen unprecedented growth and infrared thermometers have become commonplace in every household. In contrast to colourimetric and fluorescent quantification, photothermal quantification has the potential to become widespread in every household.

The aim of this work was to develop a water-soluble red carbon dot-based tandem detection sensor of “fluorescence-wavelength” dual-readout label-free POCT and “fluorescence-photothermal” dual-readout nanobody immunosensor to enable accurate detection of BSA and β-LG in food products, for the assessment of milk quality, the identification of pasteurized and UHT milk, the monitoring of milk content in milk-containing beverages, and the high-sensitivity detection of allergens in dairy-free beverages, which provide methodological basis for the detection of BSA and β-LG, thereby reducing the risk of disease or other health risks of consumers due to potential allergen intake.

β-lactoglobulin standard, κ-casein, α-lactalbumin, citric acid, urea, bovine serum albumin (BSA), chicken ovalbumin (OVA), chloroauric acid, sodium citrate, chloroplatinic acid hydrate, l-ascorbic acid were all from Sigma Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Horseradish peroxidase (HRP)-conjugated goat anti-mouse IgG, anti-His antibodies, and Sephadex G-25 Medium were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Carlsbad, CA, USA). 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) two-component chromogenic solution was purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). The anti-β-LG Nb (Nb82) and anti-β-LG mAb were made by our Lab.

3 g of citric acid and 5 g of urea were added to 10 mL of ultrapure water to form clear solution, then the solution was heated in a 750 W microwave oven for 5 min, during which time the solution changed from a colorless liquid to brown and finally to dark brown clustered solid, after cooling to room temperature, 20 mL of water was added to dissolve the product. The dissolved product was centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min, and the supernatant was purified by Sephadex G-25 Medium and green-fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs) were collected (λex/λem = 390 nm/525 nm) (Dong, et al., 2019).

CDs were synthesized according to Zhang’s work (Zhang et al. 2022a). 2 g of citric acid and 4 g of urea were added to 20 mL of formic acid (FA) to form a clear solution. Then, the mixed solution was transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor, and it was kept at 160 °C for 4 h. The resulting solution was cooled naturally to room temperature and add 40 mL of ethanol, dissolve fully, and then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 5 min and repeated 3 times.
100 μL of BSA (10%) was added to 1 mL R-CDs prepared by the method described in 2.3 . The mixed solutions were heated at 50 °C for 10 min to give rise R-CDs and BSA composites (R-CDs@BSA).

AuNPs seeds were synthesized as follows: 1 mL of chloroauric acid solution (1 %) (w/v) was added into 100 mL of ultrapure water, and heated to boiling. Then 2 mL of sodium citrate solution (2 %) (w/v) was added. Under stirring, it can be clearly observed that the solution changes from colorless to wine red. After the color stable, stirring for 15 min, resulting in a homogeneous solution. Finally, the produced AuNPs were cooled down to room temperature, made up to 100 mL with ultrapure water and stored at 4 °C for future use.

10 mL of the AuNPs solution prepared above was added in a 15 mL centrifuge tube, chloroplatinic acid hydrate, l-ascorbic acid, was mixed with the prepared AuNPs and reacted 12 h at 37 °C. After washing for several times with ultrapure water, Au@PtNPs was dispersed in water and stored at 4 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pretreatment of reaction cups for immunosensores
ce:section.ce:para: 100 μL of Nbs (0.1 μg/cup, in PBS) was added to each reaction cup and incubated at 4 °C overnight. Then, 200 μL of BSA (1%) was added after washing by PBST and incubated at 37℃ for 1 h and washed by PBST.

For detecting BSA, 250 μL of 100-fold diluted samples to be detected was placed in a clean reaction cup, to which 4 μL of 32-fold diluted R-CDs were added, then, the wavelength results of BSA were determined by measuring the fluorescence spectra in the range of 620–650 nm after mixing and the fluorescence results were determined by excitation/emission at 570/640 nm. And then, the solution was used in the β-LG detection procedure: 100 μL of sample (or 100 μL of β-LG standard solution) and 100 μL of β-LG-mAb were added to the immunosensor reaction cups after dilution of the solution and reacted for 1 h at 37 °C. After adding HRP-conjugated goat anti-mouse IgG for 30 min, R-CDs@BSA or G-CDs mixed with H2O2 -TMB solution were added to each reaction cup for 10 min at 37 °C. G-CDs fluorescence results were detected by excitation/emission at 390/525 nm, R-CDs@BSA fluorescence results were determined by excitation/emission at 570/640 nm, and photothermal results were recorded by infrared thermography after 30 s of 808 nm laser irradiation. The colorimetric, wavelength and fluorescence were detected by a Tecan SPARK® multimode microplate reader (Tecan Trading AG, Switzerland) and photothermal signals were detected using a FOTRIC 226 s infrared thermal imager (Shanghai, FOTRIC Technology Co., China). The ELISA procedure is similar to that described above without the addition of R-CDs@BSA or G-CDs.

5 mL of milk or beverage cooled at 4℃ was placed in a 15 mL centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 3000 r/min for 10 min. The solution with the fat layer discarded was diluted by PBS for several folds for detecting.

Citric acid and urea, the two most common raw materials, are often used for the synthesis of CDs (Scheme 1 a). Microwave-assisted and solvothermal methods were used to synthesize CDs from citric acid and urea, respectively, and the two CDs were identified by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 1 a-c), UV–vis spectra and fluorescence spectroscopy (Fig. 1d-i), and Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) (Fig. S1 d-f), X-ray Photoelectron spectra (XPS) (Fig. S1 g-h) and Raman spectra (Fig. S1 i). TEM images showed that CDs with an average particle size of about 4.7 ± 0.24 nm (Fig. S1a) were uniformly dispersed in the solution (Fig. 1a). The spectral results further identified G-CDs with a maximum excitation wavelength of 395 nm and emission wavelength of 525 nm with narrow and symmetric emission peaks (Fig. 1d and Fig. S2a). Meanwhile, the red fluorescent carbon dots (R-CDs) were synthesized from citric acid and urea in formic acid solution. Compared with G-CDs, R-CDs have smaller particle size in water with average particle size of 2.4 ± 0.14 nm (Fig. 1b and Fig. S1b). Similarly, the spectral results showed that the R-CDs showed a maximum visible light absorption peak at 572 nm and a slight blue shift of the maximum excitation wavelength to 560 nm, accompanied by a red fluorescence with an emission wavelength of 640 nm (Fig. 1e). FT-IR results (Fig. S1d-e) showed that both G-CDs and R-CDs had significant CO absorption bands at 1706 cm−1, and R-CDs with formic acid as a reaction aid had more OH (3430.87 cm−1 and 1355.21 cm−1) compared to G-CDs. Based on the XPS results (Fig. S1 g-i), it is clear that the addition of urea provided a nitrogen source for the CDs, with ratios of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements of 56.15:18.64:25.21 (G-CDs) and 61.84:15.36:22.8 (R-CDs), respectively. With the help of Raman spectra (Fig. S1 j-k), it was seen that G-CDs exhibit two broad features, at 1460.17 cm−1 and 1613.47 cm−1, R-CDs exhibit two broad features, at 1355.38 cm−1 and 1634.74 cm−1, close to the D (disorder) and G (graphite) bands, respectively.

In water, the fluorescence of R-CDs is very weak and almost invisible to the naked eye. However, after co-incubation with BSA, R-CDs@BSA exhibited strong fluorescence visible to the naked eye (Fig. 2 a), and the TEM results showed that the CDs were dispersed in solution in an aggregated form with average particle size of 2.3 ± 0.13 nm (Fig. 1c and S1c). Compared with the dispersed distribution of R-CDs, R-CDs@BSA exhibited a relatively aggregated distribution trend. In addition, and the introduction of BSA significantly improved the stability of R-CDs (Fig. S3). The FT-IR results (Fig. S1e-f) showed that no significant denaturation of BSA occurred during the complex synthesis, so it was speculated that R-CDs and BSA interacted with each other by physical adsorption. And compared with R-CDs, R-CDs@BSA showed a red shift in the maximum absorption peak and a blue shift maximum excitation peak (Fig. 1f). It is worth mentioning that the Ex-Em 3D map (Fig. 1g-i) shows that G-CDs, R-CDs and R-CDs@BSA all have single luminescence centers in excitation-emission maps, which means that the sp2 clusters contained in R-CDs are uniform in size and surface state(Lai, et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of “fluorescence-wavelength” dual-readout POCT for detecting BSA
ce:section.ce:para: Notably, a color shift of R-CDs from violet to blue-violet was observed with increasing BSA addition, while the fluorescence intensity gradually increased with increasing BSA addition under UV light (Fig. 2a-left). However, the maximum absorption peak of R-CDs@BSA undergoes a significant red shift from 535 nm to 565 nm (Fig. S4 a) and the maximum emission peak was blue-shifted from 645 nm to 628 nm (Fig. S4 b). According to the 1931 CIE coordinates plot, the CIE coordinates gradually changed from (0.7036, 0.2963) to (0.6999, 0.3000), where the x coordinate gradually decreased and the y coordinate gradually increased (Fig. 2a-right). This may be because the CO groups on R-CDs are in direct contact with water molecules through intermolecular hydrogen bonding, which weakens the electron-absorbing ability of the surface and leads to a rapid electron transfer burst to the excited state of water molecules (Jiang, Liu, Cheng, Yang, Jiang, Bai, et al., 2012). And with the increase of BSA, the BSA molecules could effectively prevent the contact of water molecules with the surface CO groups (Fig. 2b), which weakened the electron absorption ability of the surface and thus the charge transfer, resulting in the red-shift of the visible absorption spectrum and the blue-shift of the fluorescence spectrum. Further, the analysis of the effect of environmental pH on the BSA responsiveness of R-CDs (Fig. 2c) showed that the responsiveness of R-CDs to BSA was weaker in an acidic environment (pH < 5) and weakened with decreasing pH. This is due to the reversible denaturation of BSA under conditions below the isoelectric point of BSA, which weakens its interaction with R-CDs (Hang, Yang, Qu, & Hua, 2014).

The response properties of R-CDs to BSA were further investigated (Fig. 2d-e). Interestingly, there was not only a BSA concentration dependence of the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs, but also a significant BSA concentration dependence of their maximum emission wavelength. When BSA was added in the range of 5 μg/mL-5 mg/mL, the fluorescence of R-CDs showed a logarithmic increasing trend with the increase of BSA concentration (y = 4578.1ln(x) + 37276, R2 = 0.9928), and when BSA was added in the range of 5 μg/mL-1 mg/mL, the maximum emission wavelength of fluorescence of R-CDs showed a logarithmic decreasing trend with the increase of BSA concentration (y = -2.135ln(x) + 629.76, R2 = 0.9921). To simplify the detection procedure, the fluorescence detection condition was chosen to be 640 nm. As shown in the Fig. 2g, the POCT achieved immediate label-free detection of BSA (y = 33.24ln(x) + 179.43, R2 = 0.9941) with LOD (3σ) of 4.89 μg/mL and LOQ (10σ) of 8.94 μg/mL for fluorescence mode and LOD of 3.38 μg/mL and LOQ (10σ) of 33.64 μg/mL for wavelength mode. The detection error of wavelength readings is larger due to the detection step of fluorescence spectroscopy (1 nm), and the wavelength mode is more suitable for the calibration of detection results.

Furthermore, another interesting phenomenon is that the CDs exhibit good recognition specificity and can produce significant fluorescence only with the addition of BSA, while no fluorescence-enhancing properties are produced in the presence of β-LG, casein, skim milk powder and OVA. Combining the TEM results with the previous literature reports, it was speculated that the addition of BSA formed supra-molecular colloidal envelopes, which served to isolate water molecules from binding to CO on the surface of R-CDs, and also reduced the polarity of the system, inhibiting the rotation or vibration of fluorophores and thus enhancing the fluorescence of CDs (S. Zhang, Li, Zhou, Shi, He, Zhao, et al., 2023).

To verify the practicality of this POCT, BSA was added to PBS buffer, milk, milk containing beverage and green tea and prepared into 1 mg/mL of BSA solution and assayed, and the results are shown in Fig. S5. Firstly, BSA, PBS buffer, milk, milk containing beverage and green tea did not fluoresce in the range of 600–800 nm under the excitation of 570 nm excitation light, thus indicating that the above samples had no matrix effect on POCT. In addition, when 1 mg/mL of BSA was added to the above solutions, the fluorescence of R-CDs all recovered to the range of 37000–39000, and the maximum emission wavelength was 631 nm, which was in full agreement with the results of the standard curve in Fig. 2d-e. Different concentrations of BSA were added to milk, dairy-containing products and green tea and were diluted 100-fold by POCT to verify the accuracy and usefulness of the POCT assay, and the results are shown in Fig. 2h-i. The recovery rates of BSA in samples were 82.6–106.3% for the fluorescence mode and 72.3–107.2% for the wavelength mode. both reading modes of POCT can achieve accurate detection of BSA in food.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of “fluorescence-photothermal” dual-readout nanobody-immunosensor for detecting β-LG
ce:section.ce:para: The excitation or emission spectra of G-CDs and R-CDs@BSA were found to have overlap with oxTMB, the oxidation product of a common substrate of HRP (Fig. 3 a). Among them, the excitation spectrum of G-CDs overlaps with the absorption peak of oxTMB at 370 nm, while the emission spectrum of R-CDs@BSA completely overlaps with the absorption peak of oxTMB at 652 nm, and thus, both are thought to undergo fluorescence quenching with oxTMB due to the internal filtration effect. However, unlike other linear fluorescence quenching trends due to the internal filtration effect, both G-CDs and R-CDs@BSA exhibited a logarithmic decrease in fluorescence intensity with increasing absorbance value in the HRP-H2O2-TMB system (Fig. 3c). This was speculated that in addition to the internal filtration effect, TMB provided electrons to CDs during oxidation, which led to the quenching of CDs by electron transfer. In addition, R-CDs@BSA showed higher fluorescence quenching efficiency in this system (58.4% for G-CDs when the absorbance value was 0.49, and 83.5% for R-CDs@BSA when the absorbance value was 0.36), and they showed different logarithmic decreases trend with the cut-off point at the absorbance value of 0.1, respectively, and when the absorbance value was less than 0.1, the quenching efficiency of R-CDs@BSA was significantly higher than that for absorbance values greater than 0.1. This, in turn, was speculated that the hydroxyl radicals (·OH) generated during the oxidation of TMB by H2O2 attacked the R-CDs@BSA, forming a ·OH-dependent fluorescence quenching (J. Zhang, Wu, Thang, Tseng, Hao, Xu, et al., 2022; Yue, Zhang, Yu, Yu, Hou, Chen, et al., 2019). To test this theory, Au@Pt nanoparticles with oxidase activity (Au@PtNPs, the syntheses details are shown in ESM) were synthesized (Fig. 3b) to construct the Au@PtNPs-TMB system, and the relationship between the generation of oxTMB and the fluorescence of R-CDs@BSA was assessed by the absence of H2O2 involvement. The quenching trend of R-CDs@BSA in the absence of H2O2 remained consistent with the trend in the interval of absorbance values of 0–0.5 and was almost the same as the quenching efficiency of G-CDs (Fig. 3c). Moreover, the results of kinetic analysis also showed (Fig. S6) that the fluorescence of R-CDs@BSA underwent a significant quenching compared to G-CDs at the early stage of the oxidation reaction. These results confirmed that reactive oxygen species such as ·OH generated by H2O2 during the oxidation of TMB were involved in the quenching of R-CDs@BSA fluorescence. In addition, R-CDs@BSA had higher resistance to H2O2 interference than G-CDs (Fig. S7). Therefore, R-CDs@BSA is considered as a more suitable probe for the construction of CDs/HRP-H2O2-TMB fluorescence quenching system than G-CDs.

The β-LG Nb (Nb82) and the mAb were selected for the construction of the sELISA after optimizing (Fig. S8-S9). The sELISA achieved the highly sensitive detection of β-LG with LOD of 0.12 ng/mL and LOQ of 1.97 ng/mL (linear range of 1 ng/mL-1 μg/mL)(Fig. 3d), which was 37.9-fold higher sensitive than indirect competitive ELISA using Nb82 as detecting antibody and was 115.2-fold higher than sELISA using polyclonal antibody against β-LG and Nb82 as capturing and detecting antibody(Hu et al. 2022). In addition, the sELISA exhibited good specificity for Nb82 (Fig. 3e), which hardly recognizes proteins other than β-LG. Subsequently, G-CDs and R-CDs@BSA were used as fluorescent donor probes to construct an ·OH-dependent FQISA after optimizing (Scheme 1b and Fig. S10). According to the normalized standard curve with G-CDs and R-CDs as porbes (Fig. S11), the sensitivity of immunosensor with G-CDs as probe was significantly lower than that of R-CDs, even lower than sELISA, and the detection range was narrower. While the R-CDs showed good detection performance, which achieved highly sensitive detecting for β-LG with LOD of 0.034 ng/mL and LOQ of 0.12 ng/mL (linear range of 0.1 ng/mL-0.1 μg/mL) (Fig. 3f), which was 3.5 folds more sensitive than sELISA, and the detection range was improved by one order of magnitude.

The high absorbance of oxTMB provided the basis for the stable output of the photothermal signal. After the interaction of HRP with the H2O2-TMB system reached equilibrium (after 15 min), 1 mL of oxTMB solution was irradiated by an 808 nm laser at 1 w/cm2 (Fig. 3g). With the increase of irradiation time, the temperature of the solution gradually increased and reached the equilibrium at 8 min, and the temperature of the solution had increased from the initial temperature of 23.2 °C to 54.5 °C at 10 min, which showed that oxTMB has good photothermal conversion efficiency. Further, the trend of temperature with the absorbance of oxTMB was evaluated to judge the suitability of the photothermal signal (Fig. 3h). With the increase of HRP concentration, both the colorimetric signal and the photothermal signal showed an increasing trend, but it was obvious that when Ab652 nm was less than 0.86, the increasing trend of the photothermal signal was more obvious than the colorimetric signal, while when Ab652 nm was greater than 0.86, the colorimetric signal showed a higher trend, indicates that the sensitivity of the photothermal signal is better than the colorimetric signal at low concentrations. and it was similar to the results of Qin et al.(Qin, Chan, Boulware, Akkin, Butler, & Bischof, 2012). Based on this, PTISA was established to detect β-LG (Fig. 3i). The method achieved a highly sensitive detection of β-LG with LOD of 0.075 ng/mL and LOQ of 3.9 ng/mL (linear range of 0.1 ng/mL-0.1 μg/mL), which was 1.6 times more sensitive than that of s-ELISA.

The performance of immunosensors for sample detection was verified and compared with a commercial ELISA kit (Fig. S12). Pasteurized milk, UHT milk, milk beverages, and green tea after POCT detecting were used as samples to verify the performance of the three assays for the identification of milk by different therm-tprocesses, the monitoring of milk content in milk beverages and the highly sensitive screening of milk allergens in non-milk beverages. Thanks to the high specificity of Nbs, the samples were tested by simple dilution. The detection results of β-LG in pasteurized milk by sELISA, the immunosensor at “fluorescence” mode and “photothermal” mode was 3.084 ± 0.545 mg/mL, 3.065 ± 0.637 mg/mL, and 2.985 ± 0.622 mg/mL, which were consistent with the results of commercial ELISA kits and reports (Villamiel, Arias, Corzo, & Olano, 1999). This result further demonstrated the accuracy of the three assays. However, the concentration of β-LG in UHT milk were detected as 0.947 ± 0.113 mg/mL, 0.896 ± 0.139 mg/mL, and 0.983 ± 0.241 mg/mL, which were only 1/3 of those in pasteurized milk. It was caused by the unfolding of β-LG during high-temperature heating, modification of lysine residues at the antigen recognition site by lactose, and the formation of copolymers between casein and β-LG during heating, which reduced the antibody recognition performance (Krishna, Najda, Bains, Tosif, Papliński, Kapłan, et al., 2021) (Raynes, Day, Crepin, Horrocks, & Carver, 2017) (Keppler, Heyn, Meissner, Schrader, & Schwarz, 2019). Given that the processing conditions of milk beverages are similar to those of UHT milk, β-LG in the two milk beverage products was determined by three methods to validate the detection results of UHT milk. The β-LG in the two milk-containing beverages was monitored at 0.26–0.39 mg/mL, which is about 1/3 of the β-LG in UHT milk and is consistent with the milk-containing beverage production standard. The results further demonstrate the ability of all three assays to identify pasteurized and UHT milk and to monitor the milk content in milk beverages. In addition, green tea was selected as a milk-free beverage to evaluate the advantages of the three assays in the highly sensitive analysis of allergens. Different concentrations of UHT milk were added to the green tea and diluted to be tested (Table 1 ). All three methods can detect the addition of UHT milk to green tea with recovery of 78.3%- 85.8%, and among them, the immunosensor with “fluorescence” mode detecting the addition of as low as 0.1% UHT milk and “photothermal” mode detecting the addition of 1% UHT milk.

BSA and β-LG are important quality indicators and common allergens, and an increasing number of functional nanomaterials are being prepared and used as signal probes for the detection of BSA and β-LG (Table S1 and Table S2). Based on the unique protein structure of BSA, many fluorescent materials are used as fluorescent probes for the direct recognition of BSA, but limited by the recognition properties of the materials, these methods always have low detection sensitivity. In addition, antibodies are often used as recognition elements in combination with a variety of detection systems to achieve highly sensitive detection of BSA. Although immunoassays are highly sensitive, they are time consuming, cumbersome, and costly compared to direct recognition methods. In this study, the fluorescence intensity and maximum emission wavelength of R-CDs both vary with BSA concentration, which is rare and interesting. The label-free POCT allows for a highly specific and immediate detection of BSA in the range of 5 μg/mL to 5 mg/mL by stable CDs at almost no cost, and the development of the dual signal output mode provides more assurance of the accurate output of the detection results. However, when the method was used for the determination of BSA in pasteurized and UHT milk, background BSA values could not be detected. This may be due to the fact that the LOD of the POCT (5 μg/mL) is higher than the background value of BSA in milk (∼3.8 μg/mL). In addition, it is guessed that because pasteurised and UHT milks undergo thermal denaturation of BSA due to processing, the interaction between R-CDs and denatured BSA is weakened, which prevents effective fluorescence enhancement, similar phenomenon occurred in the BSA-protected gold clusters (Chib, et al., 2015). We will further design and optimise CDs with higher recognition performance to improve BSA detection sensitivity and explore the effect of denaturing conditions of BSA on the formation of R-CDs@BSA and its mechanism.

The detection of β-LG, on the other hand, relies on recognition probes, such as aptamers or antibodies. The high sensitivity of antibody-based immunosensors is an advantage compared to aptamers. However, the non-renewable resource of polyclonal antibodies limits its sustainable use. The “fluorescence-photothermal” dual-readout nanobody-immunosensor proposed in this work replaced polyclonal antibodies with nanobodies and monoclonal antibodies, which increases the sustainability of the method while improving the detection sensitivity (Table S2). In addition, the introduction of fluorescence and photothermal “dual-mode” signals of CDs&oxTMB, on the one hand, increases the possibility of mutual verification of “fluorescence-photothermal” signals to greatly improve the accuracy of the assay, and on the other hand, the sensor provides a choice of two signals for the detection of CDs&oxTMB, which broadens the application scenarios of the detection method and provides support for the subsequent development of portable detection technology products.

It is worth mentioning that the method requires only simple dilution of less than 5 μL of samples to achieve high sensitivity for the detection of BSA and β-LG, compared to the complex pretreatment procedures reported, and can effectively discriminate between milk with different heat treatment conditions due to the recognition selectivity of the nanobodies, which has been overlooked in most reports. Overall, our proposed sensor breaks new ground in high-sensitivity detection of BSA and β-LG and innovation in immunoassay methods.

Water-soluble green and red fluorescent carbon dots (G-CDs and R-CDs) were prepared using citric acid and urea, of which the R-CDs have BSA-dependent fluorescence enhancement properties. The fluorescence enhancement mechanism was investigated and based on this a “Fluorescence-wavelength” dual-readout label-free POCT was constructed for the specific and rapid detection of the milk quality marker BSA. In addition, R-CDs@BSA exhibited ·OH-responsive fluorescence quenching properties when interacting with the HRP-H2O2-TMB system. Compared with G-CDs, the HRP-H2O2-TMB system showed higher fluorescence quenching efficiency for R-CDs@BSA. On this basis, a “fluorescence-photothermal” dual-readout immunosensor with Nb as the capture antibody and mAb as the detection antibody was developed for the highly specific and sensitive detection of β-LG. And a “fluorescence-photothermal” dual-readout immunosensor with Nb as the capture antibody and mAb as the detection antibody was developed for the highly specific and sensitive detection of β-LG. The tandem combination of the POCT and the immunosensor has successfully enabled the simultaneous detection of BSA and β-LG in milk and the identification of milk under different thermal processing conditions, the monitoring of milk content in milk-containing beverages and the highly sensitive screening of milk allergens in non-milk beverages. This work provides an accurate methodological basis for monitoring the quality of milk products and the mislabeling of allergen components in food products and provides new support for ensuring the food safety of milk products.

Shijie Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Linqing Nie: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Lu Yang: Methodology, Data curation. Dancai Fan: Conceptualization, Methodology. Junping Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation. Yaozhong Hu: Conceptualization, Methodology. Yan Zhang: Investigation, Validation, Supervision. Shuo Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137019.
